intro:
The purpose (boule) of God refers to his eternal and comprehensive plan encompassing all things — creation, redemption, and consummation — carried out according to his foreknowledge and sovereign will. The term and concept appear prominently in the Pauline letters: 'In him we were also chosen, having been predestined according to the plan of him who works out everything in conformity with the purpose of his will' (Eph. 1:11); 'in all things God works for the good of those who love him, who have been called according to his purpose' (Rom. 8:28); 'this grace was given us in Christ Jesus before the beginning of time' (2 Tim. 1:9). The eternal character of God's purpose — planned 'before the foundation of the world' (Eph. 1:4) — establishes that redemption is not a divine contingency plan but the fulfillment of an eternal intention.

Reformed theology systematized this as the 'decree of God' — a single eternal divine act of will encompassing all of history, executed in time through secondary causes while maintaining divine sovereignty. The question of how divine purpose relates to human freedom and responsibility is the central concern of the predestination debates between Augustinian/Calvinist and Arminian/Molinist traditions. Both affirm that God has a purpose for each believer and for history as a whole; they differ on whether this purpose is unconditionally determined or conditionally responsive to foreseen human choices. Scripture presents both poles: God's purpose stands firm (Rom. 9:11; Eph. 1:11), yet humans are genuinely responsible agents — Acts 2:23 describes the crucifixion as both 'handed over to you by God's deliberate plan' and accomplished by 'wicked men' who put Jesus to death, holding divine foreordination and human culpability in paradoxical unity.
